text: The surgeon decompresses the distended gallbladder using a surgical instrument, preparing to retract it cephalad within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attempts to manipulate the gallbladder, which appears very thin, with the infundibulum obscured by dense, woody tissue. Due to the patient's large liver, the surgeon struggles to flip the gallbladder over it, repeatedly adjusting grip to improve visualization. The gallbladder is opened to drain bile, aiming to prevent visual obstruction during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a large stone lodged in the neck of the gallbladder. Using a hook cautery, they navigate through the pericholecystic fluid, which appears edematous. The ICG dye is used to enhance visualization, aiding in the identification of the critical view of safety within the triangle of Calot. The duct begins to come into view, although the stone obstructs easy manipulation of the infundibulum.

The surgeon attempts to locate anatomical landmarks as the cystic plate is fused to the liver, making visualization challenging. The stone in the gallbladder is moved, allowing the ICG to illuminate the cystic duct. The surgeon struggles to visualize the infundibulum and establish the cystic artery, using suction and irrigation to aid in the process. Due to the patient's obesity, a long trocar is used after insufflation issues with the initial trocar.

The surgeon identifies what appears to be the cystic artery and a posterior artery, carefully manipulating them to determine if they dive into the liver. The posterior gallbladder and cystic plate are visibly gangrenous, and the surgeon maintains caution around a large vessel.

The surgeon observes a vessel diving back into the liver and manages to preserve it. The cystic plate is completely gangrenous, and the gallbladder is entered, leading to reduced visualization. The gangrenous tissue is paper-thin and tears easily. The surgeon attempts to reorient on the cystic plate while managing visualization challenges.

Certainly see why I made the decision to do this at ten thirty at night. You know, this guy's a diabetic, very elevated white blood cell count, he's tachycardic. Guy did great. Went home post-op day two, gave him antibiotics for forty-eight hours. I did culture the fluid as well. You can see just a battle with visualization, just maintaining my patience throughout the night here. See, the best part of the visualization occurs in the last, you know, twenty seconds of the case. But the infundibular dissection was relatively straightforward, you know. I mean, it could have been much worse for that. I think the hardest part was just getting this thing off. and just trying to manipulate it and hold it. You got a very fatty liver. I was unable to pull it up over. And here I'm just trying to get rid of that posterior cystic portion of the gallbladder that I was left on the cystic plate. Just trying to maneuver that out without leaving that behind.

The surgeon navigates the obliterated tissue plane along the cystic plate, avoiding drifting into the liver due to the lack of discernible anatomical landmarks. The gallbladder is placed into a 12mm EndoCatch bag, requiring a larger incision for removal. The surgical field is washed out, revealing the inflamed tissue rind where the gallbladder was resting.
